story:
I remember. I always remember. I watch, and they forget. Not the ones who grew with me—wolves learning the moon, seedlings learning the sun—but the newcomers. They arrive with fear and hurry, with iron and sweat and the blood of their own and mine. They call me quiet with voices like knives. They are wrong. I am not stillness.

I am a thousand songs at once. Sit long enough and you will hear them: my daughters creaking—pine, cedar, willow—roots burrowing, leaves breaking free. Mist threads the branches of my sons and brings news of my sister, The Sea, and our far-swamp brother. Every movement adds to my chorus.

I remember the upright ones trampling ferns, etching names into bark, making my children cry. I remember the wrapper left behind, a thorn that does not rot, a small wrong that will outlast their steps. I am not cruel, even to those who do not see me. But I do not forget.

Four winters ago, a different one came. Smaller. Softer. A child, with shoes too big and no stick, no pack, no Noise. She stepped as if she knew my grain. She sat on a fallen oak—old, tired, and ready—now home to moss and mushrooms. She did not demand. Before I shaded her, before I showed her The Stream, she did the rarest thing. She gave me a gift.

She sang.

A thin, wobbly song, but true. The foxes, the crickets, even the wind learned her rhythm. Her notes threaded my branches like lightning that warms instead of burns. She left no thorn, only kindness. I chose her, and she chose me. A leaf slipped into her lap. Not an accident. My gift to carry.

She will return. She will show the leaf to someone who will not understand, but she will. Perhaps she will bring her children. Perhaps she will teach them to sit and listen, to hum with the crickets, to know that once the forest answered back. Even if it takes longer than her lifetime, I can wait. I last.

I am not silence. I am not absence. I am the song you hear only when you stop trying to own me. When you cut me, I heal—slower than you think. When you burn me, I regrow—not always the same. Every footstep writes its weight into my soil.

I do not divide my creatures into good and bad. A wolf’s hunger is not wicked. A rabbit’s terror is not tragic. They are threads in a forever weaving. Humans like stories where I am cursed or blessed. I am simply myself.

At dawn, The Sun wakes us: a jay scolds a squirrel, rain-swollen streams murmur over mice whispers, mushrooms push upward with stone patience. By day, I watch cautious deer. By night, I hold moths, owls, and beings with more eyes than names. My shadows are blankets.

I feel you when you come. You smell of smoke. Sometimes I tilt a branch, and you call it wind. The child noticed. I am waiting. She is welcome.

I am the forest. I remain when you leave. I breathe, watch, and grow. Always growing. I am not yours, and I will outlast you, as The Moon follows The Sun.
